'''Chris Sandow''' (born 9 January 1989) is an [[Indigenous Australian]] former professional [[rugby league]] footballer who played as a goal-kicking {{rlp|hb}} or {{rlp|fe}}. He could also play as a {{rlp|fb}}.<ref name="Profile at loverugbyleague.com"/><ref name="Statistics at rugbyleagueproject.org"/>

==Background==
Sandow was born in [[Kingaroy]], [[Queensland]].{{citation needed|date=May 2025}}

Sandow grew up in Queensland, playing [[rugby league]] in the [[Australian Aborigines|Aboriginal]] community of [[Cherbourg, Queensland|Cherbourg]], a three-hour drive north-west of [[Brisbane]].

He later moved to [[Brisbane]], where he attended [[Marsden State High School]] with [[Israel Folau]] and [[Antonio Winterstein]].

Sandow is from the [[Bigambul]]/[[Gubbi Gubbi people|Gubbi Gubbi]] traditional [[Australian Aborigines|Aboriginal]] territory.<ref>{{cite press release|url=http://www.nrl.com/indigenous-all-stars-team-named/tabid/10976/newsid/65573/default.aspx|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120304183916/http://www.nrl.com/indigenous-all-stars-team-named/tabid/10976/newsid/65573/default.aspx|url-status=dead|archive-date=4 March 2012|title=Indigenous All Stars team named|date=7 December 2011|access-date=14 May 2012|publisher=National Rugby League }}</ref>

[[File:Chris Sandow (24 October 2008) 5.jpg|200px|thumb|right|Sandow on duty with the [[Australian Aboriginal rugby league team|Indigenous Dreamtime]] Team.]]

==Playing career==
===Previous clubs===
He has previously played for the Moranbah Miners of the local Grade A [[Mackay, Queensland|Mackay]] [[rugby league]] competition, [[Norths Devils]] in the [[Queensland Cup]], [[Warrington Wolves]] in the [[Super League]], he has also played for the [[Parramatta Eels]] and the [[South Sydney Rabbitohs]] in the [[National Rugby League]].

===Early career===
He played Colts and [[Queensland Cup]] [[rugby league]] for the [[Souths Logan Magpies]]. Sandow received junior representative honors when he was selected for the [[Queensland]] under-17's and under-19's squads.

In 2006, while in year 12 at [[Marsden State High School]], his classmates were [[Israel Folau]] and [[Antonio Winterstein]].<ref name="School days to class acts">{{cite web|url=http://www.heraldsun.com.au/sport/nrl/school-days-to-class-acts/story-e6frfgbo-1225702277172|title=School days to class acts|work=Herald Sun|date=23 April 2009|access-date=1 January 2010}}</ref>

Sandow was selected for the [[Australian schoolboys rugby league team|Australian Schoolboys]] squad that toured [[Wales]], [[England]] and [[France]]. In which he was awarded the 'Man-of-the-Match' Award, in the game against the [[British Amateur Rugby League Association]] (BARLA) squad.

Sandow's first contract was signed with the [[Gold Coast Titans]], where he spent two-years in the lower-grades.

He was touted as a first-string back-up for star [[NRL]] {{rlp|hb}}, [[Scott Prince]]. But his contract was terminated in early 2008, due to poor discipline. It was later revealed through the media, that Sandow's partner had given birth to their first child and he was struggling with the distance away from his family.<ref name="30k">{{cite news|author=James Phelps|publisher=The Daily Telegraph (Sydney)|title=30k Buy of the Season|url=http://www.news.com.au/heraldsun/sport/nrl/story/0,27074,24013206-14823,00.html|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20080922122449/http://www.news.com.au/heraldsun/sport/nrl/story/0,27074,24013206-14823,00.html|url-status=dead|archive-date=22 September 2008|access-date=2 January 2009|date=14 July 2008}}</ref>

===Senior career===
===South Sydney (2008–11)===
Sandow's junior representative performances had not gone unnoticed, therefore, [[South Sydney Rabbitohs|South Sydney]]'s Recruitment Manager Mark Hughes, signed Sandow to a minor $30,000 contract with the club.<ref name="30k" />

Sandow began playing in the [[National Youth Competition (rugby league)|National Youth Competition]] for the [[Rabbitohs]] squad, from the beginning of the [[2008 NRL Under-20s season|2008 season]].

In June 2008, the team's star {{rlp|hb}} [[Craig Wing]], suffered a near season-ending injury. Sandow was named to replace him in South Sydney's [[NRL]] first-grade team.

At the time Sandow was to make his début the club were sitting at the bottom of the [[NRL]] competition ladder. Having slumped to 11 losses from 12 matches.

In round 13 during the [[National Rugby League season 2008|2008 season]], Sandow made his first-grade début against the [[New Zealand Warriors|Warriors]] in [[Auckland]], New Zealand at [[Mt. Smart Stadium]].

On début Sandow kicked the game-sealing field-goal in the dying seconds of the match. To record [[South Sydney Rabbitohs|Souths]]' second victory of the season, defeating the [[New Zealand Warriors|Warriors]] 35–28. This marked the first time since 1999, that South Sydney had beaten the [[New Zealand Warriors|Warriors]].

In the following match Sandow scored his first [[NRL]] career try against the [[Gold Coast Titans]]. Helping his side to a tight 24–23 victory, that saw them come off the bottom of the table for the first time all season.

Sandow's fairy-tale start to his [[NRL]] career continued a week later in round 16, when after trailing 28–4 to the [[North Queensland Cowboys]] and coming back to level the scores at 28–28, he kicked the game-clinching field-goal to win the match 29–28, in what became the second biggest comeback victory in the history of the [[National Rugby League]].

Sandow finished his début season in the [[NRL]] by winning the ''[[Dally M Awards|Dally M Award]]'' – 'Rookie of the Year Award.' He was presented with the award at the end of the 2008 campaign.<ref>{{cite news|author=Josh Massoud|publisher=The Daily Telegraph (Sydney)|title=Sandow in a dizzy rise|url=http://www.news.com.au/dailytelegraph/sport/nrl/story/0,26799,24323114-5012663,00.html|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20080914020359/http://www.news.com.au/dailytelegraph/sport/nrl/story/0,26799,24323114-5012663,00.html|url-status=dead|archive-date=14 September 2008|access-date=2 January 2009|date=10 September 2008}}</ref>

He was recognised in the ''[[National Youth Competition (rugby league)|Toyota Cup]]'' – 'Team of the Year,' as the starting {{rlp|hb}}. He also received the Inaugural ''[[George Green (rugby league)|George Green]] Medal'' – 'Indigenous Rising Star' of 2008.<ref>{{cite news|author=Steve Jancetic|publisher=The Daily Telegraph (Sydney)|title=Chris Sandow wins World Cup spot, indigenous rising star medal|url=http://www.news.com.au/couriermail/sport/nrl/story/0,26746,24275265-5016289,00.html|archive-url=https://archive.today/20121230235002/http://www.news.com.au/couriermail/sport/nrl/story/0,26746,24275265-5016289,00.html|url-status=dead|archive-date=30 December 2012|access-date=2 January 2009|date=31 August 2008}}</ref>

===Parramatta Eels (2012–15)===
In 2012, Sandow signed a four-year deal with the [[Parramatta Eels]], "Reportedly" worth between $1.6 and $2.2 million.{{citation needed|date=May 2025}}

Sandow stated that the reason he was leaving South Sydney to ensure his children were, "well provided for in the future".<ref name="Chris Sandow signs with Parramatta after meeting his 'Alan Bond'">{{cite web|url=http://www.smh.com.au/rugby-league/league-news/chris-sandow-signs-with-parramatta-after-meeting-his-alan-bond-20110503-1e78w.html|title=Chris Sandow signs with Parramatta after meeting his 'Alan Bond'|work=The Sydney Morning Herald|date=4 May 2011|access-date=1 January 2012}}</ref>

Sandow's first two-years with the Parramatta Eels proved to be a struggle as his performances were inconsistent and he spent time in the [[New South Wales Cup]], with the [[Wentworthville Magpies]].<ref name="MVPs of 2014: Chris Sandow">{{cite web|url=http://www.nrl.com/mvps-of-2014-chris-sandow/tabid/10874/newsid/76078/default.aspx|title=MVPs of 2014: Chris Sandow|date=3 February 2014 |publisher=nrl.com|access-date=1 January 2018}}</ref>

In the 2012 season, Sandow was accused by [[Parramatta Eels|Parramatta]] Legend [[Peter Sterling (rugby league commentator)|Peter Sterling]] as being "overweight and unfit".<ref name="Sterling slams Parramatta halfback Chris Sandow as being overweight">{{cite web|url=http://www.dailytelegraph.com.au/sport/nrl/sterling-slams-overweight-sandow/news-story/b649881b6ab4d3cc9c9a0b0a5955f30b|title=Sterling slams Parramatta halfback Chris Sandow as being overweight|work=The Daily Telegraph|location=Australia|date=10 April 2012 |access-date=1 January 2013}}</ref>

In the 2014 season, Sandow won the ''[[Ken Thornett]] Medal'', for being voted as the Parramatta Eels' 'Player of the Season.' The 2015 season would prove to be Sandow's final year at the club.{{citation needed|date=May 2025}}

After a number of on-the field and off-the field incidents. Including but not limiting crashing a loan-car and abandoning it in an industrial area. Calling out coach [[Brad Arthur]] and being critical of his coaching methods publicly, typing a foul-mouthed rant on [[Social Media]] platform [[Facebook]] and gambling issues.<ref name="Chris Sandow exits Parramatta Eels: the good, bad and ugly from an NRL career that’s split opinion">{{cite web|url=https://www.foxsports.com.au/nrl/nrl-premiership/chris-sandow-exits-parramatta-eels-the-good-bad-and-ugly-from-an-nrl-career-thats-split-opinion/news-story/8b66fa20541f759c65338cb711e48043|title=Chris Sandow exits Parramatta Eels: the good, bad and ugly from an NRL career that's split opinion|publisher=foxsports.com.au|date=24 July 2015 |access-date=1 January 2016}}</ref><ref name="Chris Sandow may have played last game for Parramatta Eels as player swap goes awry">{{cite web|url=http://www.smh.com.au/rugby-league/chris-sandow-may-have-played-last-game-for-parramatta-eels-as-player-swap-goes-awry-20150512-ggzwre.html|title=Chris Sandow may have played last game for Parramatta Eels as player swap goes awry|work=The Sydney Morning Herald|date=31 December 2017|access-date=1 January 2018}}</ref>

Sandow's final match for the Parramatta Eels was in round 19, against arch-rivals the [[Canterbury-Bankstown Bulldogs]]. Afterwards Sandow was released by the Parramatta Eels club and "reportedly" received a $50,000 handshake.<ref name="Chris Sandow released by Parramatta Eels effective immediately">{{cite web|url=http://www.abc.net.au/news/2015-07-23/eels-release-chris-sandow/6644112|title=Chris Sandow released by Parramatta Eels effective immediately|publisher=Australian Broadcasting Corporation|date=31 December 2017|access-date=1 January 2018}}</ref><ref name="Chris Sandow for sale: The NRL clubs most likely to revive the cheeky halfback’s career">{{cite web|url=https://www.foxsports.com.au/nrl/nrl-premiership/chris-sandow-for-sale-the-nrl-clubs-most-likely-to-revive-the-cheeky-halfbacks-career/news-story/e0b31f808230d0b1ed010a223f65e995|title=Chris Sandow for sale: The NRL clubs most likely to revive the cheeky halfback's career|publisher=foxsports.com.au|date=31 December 2017|access-date=1 January 2018}}</ref>

===Warrington Wolves (2015–16)===
Sandow joined the [[Warrington Wolves]] at the start of the 2015 [[Super League Super 8s]] system. Going on to make his début against the [[Leeds Rhinos]] in a 49–10 defeat.

Sandow struggled in his opening games, however he grew more and more into the games as they wore on.{{citation needed|date=May 2025}}

In round 1 of the 2016 [[Super League]] season, Sandow scored his first try for Warrington in a 12–10 win away at the Leeds Rhinos.

He followed up in round 3, scoring his first try at the [[Halliwell Jones Stadium]] in a 36–14 win against the [[Wakefield Trinity|Wakefield Trinity Wildcats]].

Sandow was already the early front-runner to win the 2016 ''[[Man of Steel Awards|Steve Prescott Man of Steel Award]]'', with Head Coach [[Tony Smith (rugby league born 1967)|Tony Smith]] saying, "Sandow has the best kicking game in the [[Super League]] since [[Sean Long]]".<ref name="Eels Release Sandow">{{cite web|url=http://www.parraeels.com.au/news/2015/07/23/eels_release_sandow.html|title=Eels Release Sandow|date=31 December 2017|access-date=1 January 2018}}</ref>

Sandow represented the Warrington Wolves in both the [[2016 Challenge Cup Final]] defeat by [[Hull F.C.]] at [[Wembley Stadium]]<ref name="Hull FC’s Jamie Shaul’s late try takes Challenge Cup away from Warrington">{{cite web|url=https://www.theguardian.com/sport/2016/aug/27/hull-warrington-challenge-cup-final-match-report|title=Hull FC's Jamie Shaul's late try takes Challenge Cup away from Warrington|work=The Guardian|date=27 August 2016|access-date=7 November 2019}}</ref><ref name="Challenge Cup final: Hull FC 12-10 Warrington Wolves">{{cite web|url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/sport/rugby-league/37169016|title=Challenge Cup final: Hull FC 12–10 Warrington Wolves|publisher=BBC|date=27 August 2016|access-date=7 November 2019}}</ref> and the [[2016 Super League Grand Final]] defeat by the [[Wigan Warriors]] at [[Old Trafford]]<ref name="Warrington 6-12 Wigan: Super League Grand Final – as it happened!">{{cite web|url=https://www.theguardian.com/sport/live/2016/oct/08/super-league-grand-final-warrington-wigan-live|title=Warrington 6–12 Wigan: Super League Grand Final – as it happened!|work=The Guardian|date=8 October 2016|access-date=7 November 2019}}</ref> where he finished a runner-up in both major finals.

In November 2016, Sandow announced that he wasn't going to be returning to Warrington for the 2017 season.<ref>{{cite press release|url=https://warringtonwolves.com/club-news/sandow-leaves-wolves|access-date=22 November 2016|date=21 November 2016|publisher=Warrington Wolves|title=Sandow Leaves Wolves}}</ref>

In light of Sandow's statement, Warrington, were said to be, "retaining Sandow's registration until further notice".{{citation needed|date=May 2025}}

===Norths Devils (2017)===
In 2017, Sandow signed with [[Queensland Cup]] team [[Norths Devils]] on a one-year deal. Sandow played 12 games for the club, scoring three tries and kicking six goals before being stood-down from his duties due to missing a training session, subsequently leading to his departure from the club.

===Moranbah Miners (2018)===
On 20 December 2017, Sandow signed a contract to join the Moranbah Miners in the local grade A [[Mackay, Australia|Mackay]] [[rugby league]] competition for the 2018 season.<ref name="Moranbah mines for former NRL stars">{{cite web|url=https://www.dailymercury.com.au/news/moranbah-mines-for-former-nrl-stars/3295524|title=Moranbah mines for former NRL stars|publisher=dailymercury.com.au|date=31 December 2017|access-date=1 January 2018}}</ref><ref name="Tough test: NRL star to lead Miners against Yeppoon ">{{cite web|url=https://www.themorningbulletin.com.au/news/tough-test-nrl-star-to-lead-miners-against-yeppoon/3341154|title=Tough test: NRL star to lead Miners against Yeppoon|publisher=themorningbulletin.com.au|date=31 December 2017|access-date=1 January 2018}}</ref>

===Representative career (2008–12)===
Sandow earned selection in the [[Indigenous All Stars (rugby league)|Indigenous All Stars]] team, that defeated the [[New Zealand Māori rugby league team|New Zealand Māori]] 34–26, in a curtain-raising game at the [[2008 Rugby League World Cup|Rugby League World Cup]] Opening Ceremony in October 2008.<ref>{{cite news|publisher=The Vibe|title=Dreamtime Team defeat Maori 34–26|url=http://www.vibe.com.au/vibe.asp?PageID=2338|access-date=2 January 2009|date=27 October 2008|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20091020022533/https://www.vibe.com.au/vibe.asp?PageID=2338|archive-date=20 October 2009}}</ref>

Sandow played for the [[Prime Minister's XIII]] in 2010. Making only one appearance and scoring a single try.

[[File:Chris Sandow (24 October 2008) 4.jpg|200px|thumb|right|Sandow training with the [[Australian Aboriginal rugby league team|Indigenous Dreamtime]] Team.]]

==Boxing==
Sandow made his professional boxing debut on 27 November 2013 in a fight against [[Todd Carney]].

==Controversy==
On 2 January 2017, Sandow was charged with a public nuisance offence after being filmed in a street brawl in Queensland's [[South Burnett Region]].<ref name="Chris Sandow charged over street fight">{{cite web|url=http://wwos.nine.com.au/2017/01/02/19/24/chris-sandow-charged-over-street-fight|title=Chris Sandow charged over street fight|publisher=nine.com.au|date=31 December 2017|access-date=1 January 2018}}</ref>

On 10 January 2017, Sandow was fined $300 at Murgon Magistrates Court in Queensland over the brawl and he escaped conviction.<ref name="Ex-NRL star Chris Sandow fined over public brawl">{{cite web|url=http://www.smh.com.au/rugby-league/league-news/exnrl-star-chris-sandow-fined-over-public-brawl-20170110-gtopiv.html|title=Ex-NRL star Chris Sandow fined over public brawl|work=The Sydney Morning Herald|date=31 December 2017|access-date=1 January 2018}}</ref>

In September 2022, Sandow appeared in court over charges of serious assault of a police officer causing bodily harm, serious assault of a police officer or person acting in aid of police officer and two counts of obstructing police. He also faced charges of dangerous operation of a motor vehicle, two counts of driving without a licence and evasion.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.warringtonguardian.co.uk/news/21398085.former-warrington-wolves-star-chris-sandow-rejected-jail-bail/|title=Former Warrington Wolves star Chris Sandow rejected|website=www.warringtonguardian.co.uk}}</ref>

On 14 December 2024, it was reported that he had been sentenced for obstructing police after earlier being disqualified from driving. Sandow was sentenced to 200 hours of unpaid community services within a year and disqualified him from holding or obtaining a driver's licence for two years.<ref>{{cite web|website=Total RL|author=Callum Walker|date=14 Dec 2024|title=Ex-Super League and NRL star Chris Sandow sentenced after obstructing police|url=https://www.totalrl.com/ex-super-league-and-nrl-star-chris-sandow-sentenced-after-obstructing-police/}}</ref>

On 29 August 2025, Sandow was sentenced at Ipswich Magistrates Court in relation to charges involving carrying a weapon in public, drug possession, possessing explosives, assaulting and obstructing police officers, and wilful damage. Sandow was involved in throwing rocks at cars in March 2025. Sandow will be eligible for release to parole on 3 March 2026.<ref>{{cite web|website=Zero Tackle|author=Zero Tackle|date=31 August 2025|title=Former NRL player sentenced to prison|url=https://www.zerotackle.com/former-nrl-player-sentenced-to-prison-226119/}}</ref>

*[[National Rugby League season 2008|2008]]: [[George Green (rugby league)|George Green Medal]] – 'Indigenous Rising Star of 2008'
*[[2008 NRL Under-20s season|2008]]: [[National Youth Competition (rugby league)|Toyota Cup]] – 'Halfback of the Year'
*[[2014 Parramatta Eels season|2014]]: [[Ken Thornett]] Medal – [[Parramatta Eels]]' 'Player of the Season'
